title: Pride and Prejudice
author: Austen, Jane
summary: Elizabeth Bennet navigates manners, marriage, and misunderstandings in early 19th-century England. When wealthy Mr. Bingley arrives, his attachment to Jane Bennet and his friend Mr. Darcy’s proud reserve set social wheels turning. False reports, pride, and personal prejudices complicate courtships: Wickham’s deceptive charm, Mr. Collins’s officious proposals, and Lydia’s reckless elopement force families into crisis. Through wit and moral perception, Elizabeth moves from aversion to love, and misjudgments are corrected. Social rank, hypocrisy, and genuine feeling are tested before suitable matches are reached and familial reputations are restored.

characters.name: Elizabeth Bennet
characters.lifespan: marries Mr. Darcy by novel's end; settles at Pemberley (fate: married life)

characters.name: Jane Bennet
characters.lifespan: becomes engaged/marries Mr. Bingley (happy match)

characters.name: Mr. Bennet
characters.lifespan: remains head of the Bennet family; survives events of the story

characters.name: Mrs. Bennet
characters.lifespan: achieves her aim of advantageous marriages for daughters

characters.name: Lydia Bennet
characters.lifespan: elopes with Wickham; their marriage brings scandal but is arranged to avoid ruin

characters.name: Mary Bennet
characters.lifespan: remains at home, continuing moral and musical pursuits

characters.name: Kitty (Catherine) Bennet
characters.lifespan: stays within family circle; influenced by Lydia's behavior

characters.name: Mr. Darcy
characters.lifespan: overcomes pride and his earlier conduct is explained; marries Elizabeth

characters.name: Mr. Bingley
characters.lifespan: courts and marries Jane Bennet

characters.name: Mr. Wickham
characters.lifespan: elopes with Lydia and marries her; his misdeeds partly remedied by marriage

characters.name: Mr. Collins
characters.lifespan: proposes first to Jane then to Elizabeth; marries Charlotte Lucas (in novel)

characters.name: Charlotte Lucas
characters.lifespan: marries Mr. Collins (practical alliance)

characters.name: Miss Bates
characters.lifespan: remains a spinster dependent on family kindness; prominent in social scenes

characters.name: Mrs. Norris
characters.lifespan: remains a consistently unsympathetic figure in her novel

characters.name: Fanny (Price)
characters.lifespan: character outcome is discussed in preface; not central to this novel

characters.name: Edmund (Bertram)
characters.lifespan: marriage to Fanny mentioned in critical discussion

characters.name: Frank Churchill
characters.lifespan: involved in intrigues and misunderstandings (referenced)

characters.name: Jane Fairfax
characters.lifespan: her entanglements are mentioned as part of Emma’s intrigue discussion

characters.name: John Thorpe
characters.lifespan: example of a character compared in preface

characters.name: Mrs. Musgrove
characters.lifespan: used as example in critical argument

characters.name: Mr. Shapely
characters.personality: object of comic affection in Spectator
characters.lifespan: appears only in quoted comparison

characters.name: Mr. Spectator
characters.lifespan: appears as a referenced literary voice

characters.name: Jane Austen (author)
characters.age: died 1817 (preface notes published 1813, death 4 years later)
characters.lifespan: author; completed the novels and died shortly after publication

characters.name: George Saintsbury
characters.lifespan: writer of the included preface

characters.name: Hugh Thomson
characters.lifespan: provided illustrations for this edition

characters.name: J. Comyns Carr
characters.personality: friend and advisor to Hugh Thomson
characters.lifespan: acknowledged in inscription

characters.name: Mr. Goldwin Smith
characters.lifespan: cited for commentary in preface

locations.name: Charing Cross Road (156 Charing Cross Road / Ruskin House), London
Tooks Court, Chancery Lane (Chiswick Press reference), London

unusual_words.definition: vivacity, spirit; animation

unusual_words.definition: a striking or notable event (here French 'coup' as in 'coup de théâtre')
unusual_words.context: in the phrase 'coup de théâtre' describing a theatrical turn of events

unusual_words.definition: theatre (French)
unusual_words.context: used in the French phrase 'coup de théâtre' to mean dramatic event

unusual_words.definition: a literary or dramatic work that seeks to ridicule by exaggeration

unusual_words.definition: a humorous or satirical imitation
unusual_words.context: Northanger Abbey characterized as a parody of gothic novels

unusual_words.definition: captivating; highly engaging
unusual_words.context: used of Persuasion as 'relatively faint in tone, and not enthralling in interest'

unusual_words.definition: a disastrous conclusion; here used in dramatic/critical sense
unusual_words.context: describes the ending of Mansfield Park as a theatrical 'catastrophe'

unusual_words.definition: lacking flavor, vigor, or interest; dull
unusual_words.context: used to criticize hero and heroine of Mansfield Park as 'insipid'

unusual_words.definition: in a wicked or morally wrong manner

unusual_words.definition: scenes depicting rehearsals (e.g., theatrical or musical practice)

unusual_words.definition: the final resolution of a narrative

unusual_words.definition: the act of running away to get married, typically secretly

unusual_words.definition: the arrival or coming of something or someone

unusual_words.definition: not showy or pretentious; modest

unusual_words.definition: performed with skill and mastery

unusual_words.definition: a child's game of concealment and seeking; used metaphorically for contrived plotting

unusual_words.definition: to-and-fro movement; used to describe contrived narrative motions
unusual_words.context: paired with 'hide-and-seek' to characterize certain plot mechanics

unusual_words.definition: a secret or underhand scheme; complicated plot
unusual_words.context: refers to plot complexities in Emma as part of its intrigue

unusual_words.definition: a failure to understand; often used as a plot device
unusual_words.context: Austen's preference for misunderstandings as narrative opportunities

unusual_words.definition: a state of discomfort or clumsiness (social)
unusual_words.context: describes awkwardness arising from Elizabeth's changing feelings

unusual_words.definition: a thorough or dramatic change in form or character

unusual_words.definition: a strong dislike
unusual_words.context: Elizabeth’s initial aversion to Darcy shifts to affection

unusual_words.definition: a person who writes plays
unusual_words.context: speculates whether a dramatist's hand would have altered the novel

unusual_words.definition: stage lights at the front of a theatre
unusual_words.context: says the novel is unlikely to be adapted for 'footlights' (the stage)

unusual_words.definition: the lowest seating in a theatre near the stage
unusual_words.context: paired with 'gallery' to denote full theatrical audiences

unusual_words.definition: upper seating in a theatre
unusual_words.context: used with 'pit' to contrast sections of a theatre audience

unusual_words.definition: loose or weak points in construction
unusual_words.context: notes the novel lacks 'loosenesses of construction'

unusual_words.definition: advantages or devices used by novelists

unusual_words.definition: excessively critical

unusual_words.definition: branch of philosophy; capitalized as a field

unusual_words.definition: faithful representation of reality

unusual_words.definition: natural, not contrived

unusual_words.definition: unnecessary, excessive
unusual_words.context: stresses that nothing is superfluous in Austen's prose

unusual_words.definition: comparative for tall; used in phrase 'taller by the breadth of my nail' quoted from Swift

unusual_words.definition: width; used metaphorically in the Swift quotation

unusual_words.definition: in the manner of Jonathan Swift; satirical and cutting

unusual_words.definition: too great or extreme to be described in words

unusual_words.definition: expressive and persuasive (word used elsewhere in the discussion)

unusual_words.definition: the ability to see, hear, or become aware of; understanding

unusual_words.definition: figurative for darker sides of human nature

unusual_words.definition: belonging to the same period

unusual_words.definition: savagely fierce

unusual_words.definition: a supporter or benefactor (implied in publishing context)
unusual_words.context: implicit in discussion of friends and patrons like publishers and illustrators

unusual_words.definition: in a dull or bland manner (adverb form derivable from 'insipid')
unusual_words.context: adverbial sense tied to criticism of characters as insipid

unusual_words.definition: to fill with delight or wonder (related to 'entrain' used earlier)
unusual_words.context: used in discussion of freshness and charm of Austen's work

unusual_words.definition: representing the most perfect example of a quality
unusual_words.context: labels Pride and Prejudice as the 'quintessential' Austen novel
